nativeRange.habitat: Wet meadows, marshy grasslands, and riverbanks of the Rio de la Plata basin; tolerates seasonally inundated to permanently moist soils in full sun to part shade

decorPreferences: root tabs placed near (not on top of) the bulb
planted in clusters for a grassy-field effect
bulb partially buried with top exposed above the substrate

summary: A small South American bulb plant in the amaryllis family that sends up long, thin, grass-like leaves from an onion-shaped bulb; planted in clusters it creates a grassy savanna-field effect in the midground or background of medium planted tanks.
careNotes: Zephyranthes candida is a true bulb plant in the amaryllis family (Amaryllidaceae) sold in the aquarium hobby as a smaller alternative to the much larger Crinum species (compare fw-plant-061 / fw-plant-062). In wild populations of the Rio de la Plata basin (Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay, southern Brazil) it grows in seasonally inundated wet meadows; in aquariums it is treated as a fully submerged ornamental and forms long, slender, grass-like deep-green leaves roughly 3 mm wide that arch and sway in the current. Typical submerged adult height is around 15–30 cm (6–12 in per Modern Aquarium), with vigorous specimens occasionally reaching 40 cm+ under strong light and rich substrate. CRITICAL bulb-planting note: do NOT bury the bulb completely. Push the bottom half of the bulb into the substrate and leave the top half (where the leaves emerge) clearly visible above the substrate line — fully buried bulbs commonly rot before they establish. Once positioned, avoid moving or rescaping the bulb; repeated disturbance significantly delays rooting. Lighting demand is moderate; CO2 is not required but will accelerate growth and improve leaf density. The plant is a root feeder — use a nutrient-rich substrate or place root tabs in the substrate NEAR the bulb (not directly underneath or on top of it). Temperature tolerance is broad (68–82 °F / 20–28 °C) so it suits standard tropical community tanks; the pH range is not specifically published for submerged culture, so the typical neutral planted-tank window of 6.5–7.5 is given. Best visual impact comes from planting several bulbs in a cluster as a midground-to-background grassy field rather than as isolated single specimens.
breedingNotes: Propagates primarily by bulb division: mature bulbs produce daughter offsets ('pups') around the parent bulb that can be gently separated and replanted once they carry their own roots and leaves. Wild and emersed plants also reproduce sexually via white flowers that develop into capsule fruits with viable seeds, but flowering is uncommon under fully submerged aquarium conditions and seed propagation is essentially never used in the hobby.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium — Dwarf Water Onion (Zephyranthes candida)
primary.notes: Primary aquarium-husbandry source. Confirmed South American origin, submerged adult size 6–12 in (15–30 cm), temperature 68–82 °F (20–28 °C), moderate lighting, CO2 not required, slow-to-moderate growth, midground/background placement, easy-to-moderate care level, nutrient-rich substrate with root tabs placed NEAR (not on) the bulb, and the critical instruction that the bulb must be only partially buried with the top exposed to avoid rot.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Zephyranthes candida
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Amaryllidaceae, order Asparagales; described by Lindley 1823 as Amaryllis candida, transferred to Zephyranthes by Herbert 1826), native countries (Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay, Brazil — Rio de la Plata basin), leaf morphology (deep glossy green, ~3 mm wide), and propagation modes (bulb division/offsets, plus seed from capsule fruits in flowering specimens).
